content:
# Luke Chapter 17 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 109-110 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_017_page_001.jpg, LUK_017_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And he said to his disciples, "It is impossible that stumbling-blocks should not come, but woe to him through whom they come.

**2** It would be better for him if a millstone were hung around his neck and he were cast into the sea, than that he should cause one of these little ones to stumble.

**3** Take heed to yourselves. If your brother should sin, rebuke him; and if he should repent, forgive him.

**4** And if seven times in the day he should sin against you, and seven times should turn back to you, saying, 'I repent,' you shall forgive him."

**5** And the apostles said to the Lord, "Add faith to us."

**6** And the Lord said, "If you had faith as a grain of mustard seed, you would say to this mulberry tree, 'Be uprooted and be planted in the sea,' and it would obey you.

**7** But which of you, having a servant plowing or tending sheep, will say to him when he has come in from the field, 'Come at once and recline at table'?

**8** Will he not rather say to him, 'Prepare what I may eat, and gird yourself and serve me while I eat and drink, and after these things you shall eat and drink'?

**9** Does he have gratitude toward that servant because he did the things commanded? I think not.

**10** So also you, when you have done all the things commanded you, say, 'We are unprofitable servants; we have done that which we were obligated to do.'"

**11** And it came to pass, as he was going to Jerusalem, that he was passing through the midst of Samaria and Galilee.

**12** And as he entered into a certain village, ten leprous men met him, who stood at a distance.

**13** And they lifted up their voices, saying, "Jesus, Master, have mercy on us."

**14** And having seen them, he said to them, "Go, show yourselves to the priests." And it came to pass, as they were going, they were cleansed.

**15** And one of them, having seen that he was healed, turned back, with a loud voice glorifying God,

**16** and he fell upon his face at his feet, giving thanks to him. And he was a Samaritan.

**17** And Jesus, answering, said, "Were not the ten cleansed? But where are the nine?

**18** Were none found returning to give glory to God except this foreigner?"

**19** And he said to him, "Rise and go; your faith has saved you."

**20** And having been asked by the Pharisees when the kingdom of God is coming, he answered them and said, "The kingdom of God does not come with observation;

**21** nor will they say, 'Behold, here!' or 'There!' For behold, the kingdom of God is in the midst of you."

**22** And he said to the disciples, "Days will come when you will desire to see one of the days of the Son of Man, and you will not see it.

**23** And they will say to you, 'Behold, there!' or 'Behold, here!' Do not go away, nor pursue.

**24** For as the lightning, when it flashes out of one part under heaven, shines to the other part under heaven, so shall the Son of Man be in his day.

**25** But first he must suffer many things and be rejected by this generation.

**26** And just as it came to pass in the days of Noah, so also shall it be in the days of the Son of Man.

**27** They were eating, they were drinking, they were marrying, they were being given in marriage, until the day when Noah entered into the ark, and the flood came and destroyed them all.

**28** Likewise, just as it came to pass in the days of Lot — they were eating, they were drinking, they were buying, they were selling, they were planting, they were building —

**29** but on the day when Lot went out from Sodom, it rained fire and brimstone from heaven and destroyed them all.

**30** According to these things shall it be on the day when the Son of Man is revealed.

**31** In that day, let him who is on the housetop, with his goods in the house, not come down to take them up; and likewise let him who is in the field not turn back to the things behind.

**32** Remember the wife of Lot.

**33** Whoever should seek to preserve his life shall lose it, and whoever shall lose it shall preserve it alive.

**34** I say to you, in that night there shall be two upon one bed; the one shall be taken and the other shall be left.

**35** There shall be two women grinding together; the one shall be taken and the other shall be left."

**36** [This verse is absent from Codex Sinaiticus.]

**37** And answering, they say to him, "Where, Lord?" And he said to them, "Where the body is, there also will the eagles be gathered together."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Codex Sinaiticus)

1. **17:1 — "stumbling-blocks" (ta skandala):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΑ ΣΚΑΝΔΑΛΑ. The word ΣΚΑΝΔΑΛΟΝ originally denoted the trigger-stick of a trap — the mechanism that causes the trap to snap shut. In the New Testament it acquires the extended meaning of anything that causes someone to fall into sin or apostasy. The adjective ΑΝΕΝΔΕΚΤΟΝ ("impossible, inadmissible") occurs only here in the New Testament, formed from Α-privative + ΕΝΔΕΚΤΟΣ ("acceptable"). The construction ΑΝΕΝΔΕΚΤΟΝ ΕΣΤΙΝ ΤΟΥ ΤΑ ΣΚΑΝΔΑΛΑ ΜΗ ΕΛΘΕΙΝ ("it is impossible that stumbling-blocks should not come") uses the articular infinitive with genitive — a classical construction affirming the inevitability of scandal while pronouncing woe upon its human agents. The tension between divine sovereignty (stumbling-blocks must come) and human culpability (woe to the agent) is preserved without resolution.

2. **17:2 — "a millstone" (lithos mylikos):** Sinaiticus reads ΛΙΘΟΣ ΜΥΛΙΚΟΣ. The adjective ΜΥΛΙΚΟΣ ("pertaining to a mill") modifies ΛΙΘΟΣ ("stone"), denoting the large upper millstone turned by a donkey (as opposed to the small hand-mill stone used by women). The image is of an enormous stone — too heavy for a man to lift — fastened around the neck (ΠΕΡΙΚΕΙΤΑΙ ΠΕΡΙ ΤΟΝ ΤΡΑΧΗΛΟΝ ΑΥΤΟΥ) before the person is hurled into the sea. The comparative ΛΥΣΙΤΕΛΕΙ ("it would be profitable, it would be better") occurs only here in the New Testament, from ΛΥΩ + ΤΕΛΟΣ ("releasing from cost") — literally, "it pays off more." The grotesque image asserts that violent death by drowning is preferable to causing a ΜΙΚΡΟΝ ("little one") to stumble. The ΤΩΝ ΜΙΚΡΩΝ ΤΟΥΤΩΝ ("these little ones") likely refers not to children but to vulnerable believers.

3. **17:3-4 — "If your brother should sin ... seven times" (ean hamartē ho adelphos sou ... heptakis):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΑΝ ΑΜΑΡΤΗ Ο ΑΔΕΛΦΟΣ ΣΟΥ ΕΠΙΤΙΜΗΣΟΝ ΑΥΤΩ ΚΑΙ ΕΑΝ ΜΕΤΑΝΟΗΣΗ ΑΦΕΣ ΑΥΤΩ. The sequence is precise: sin prompts rebuke (ΕΠΙΤΙΜΗΣΟΝ, aorist imperative — a decisive act), repentance prompts forgiveness (ΑΦΕΣ, aorist imperative — a decisive release). Verse 4 escalates: ΕΠΤΑΚΙΣ ΤΗΣ ΗΜΕΡΑΣ ("seven times in the day") is hyperbolic — the point is not a numerical limit but the obliteration of any limit. The verb ΕΠΙΣΤΡΕΨΗ ("should turn back") uses the language of conversion (ΕΠΙΣΤΡΕΦΩ) for the act of returning to seek forgiveness. Sinaiticus reads ΕΙΣ ΣΕ ("against you") in verse 4, making the offense personal and the forgiveness costly. The imperative ΑΦΗΣΕΙΣ ("you shall forgive") in Sinaiticus uses the future indicative with imperatival force — a command clothed in the certainty of compliance.

4. **17:5 — "Add faith to us" (prosthes hēmin pistin):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΡΟΣΘΕΣ ΗΜΙΝ ΠΙΣΤΙΝ. The verb ΠΡΟΣΤΙΘΗΜΙ ("to add to, to increase") treats faith as a quantifiable commodity that can be augmented. The apostles' request assumes that more faith is the answer to the demands of verses 3-4 — that unlimited forgiveness requires superhuman spiritual resources. The title ΟΙ ΑΠΟΣΤΟΛΟΙ ("the apostles") rather than ΟΙ ΜΑΘΗΤΑΙ ("the disciples") is noteworthy; Luke reserves ΑΠΟΣΤΟΛΟΣ for the Twelve in their commissioned capacity, suggesting they speak here in their official role.

5. **17:6 — "this mulberry tree" (tē sykaminō tautē):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΗ ΣΥΚΑΜΙΝΩ ΤΑΥΤΗ. The ΣΥΚΑΜΙΝΟΣ is the black mulberry tree (Morus nigra), not to be confused with the ΣΥΚΟΜΟΡΕΑ (sycamore-fig) of 19:4. The mulberry was known for its extensive, tenacious root system — extraordinarily difficult to uproot. Jesus's response reframes the apostles' request: the issue is not the quantity of faith but its quality. ΩΣ ΚΟΚΚΟΝ ΣΙΝΑΠΕΩΣ ("as a grain of mustard seed") invokes the proverbially smallest seed. Even faith of the smallest genuine measure could command the deeply-rooted mulberry to ΕΚΡΙΖΩΘΗΤΙ ΚΑΙ ΦΥΤΕΥΘΗΤΙ ΕΝ ΤΗ ΘΑΛΑΣΣΗ ("be uprooted and be planted in the sea") — an absurd transplantation, since trees do not grow in the sea. The hyperbole dismantles the quantitative thinking of the apostles' request.

6. **17:7-10 — "unprofitable servants" (douloi achreioi):** Sinaiticus reads ΔΟΥΛΟΙ ΑΧΡΕΙΟΙ. The parable of the Unworthy Servant is unique to Luke. The rhetorical question ΤΙΣ ΔΕ ΕΞ ΥΜΩΝ ("which of you") draws the audience into the slaveholder's perspective — they are expected to identify with the master, not the servant. The verb ΑΡΟΤΡΙΩΝΤΑ ("plowing") and ΠΟΙΜΑΙΝΟΝΤΑ ("tending sheep") represent the two primary agricultural occupations. The sequence is telling: after a full day's field labor, the servant must then prepare and serve the master's meal, eat afterward, and expect no special thanks. ΜΗ ΕΧΕΙ ΧΑΡΙΝ ΤΩ ΔΟΥΛΩ ("does he have gratitude toward that servant?") expects a negative answer — the servant has merely fulfilled his obligation. The application (v. 10) uses ΑΧΡΕΙΟΣ ("unprofitable, useless") — not a moral condemnation but a statement of status: the servant creates no surplus merit. ΕΠΟΙΗΣΑΜΕΝ Ο ΩΦΕΙΛΟΜΕΝ ΠΟΙΗΣΑΙ ("we have done what we were obligated to do") frames all obedience as debt-service, not meritorious achievement. This parable corrects any presumption that the extraordinary forgiveness of vv. 3-4 or the faith of v. 6 earns divine reward.

7. **17:11 — "through the midst of Samaria and Galilee" (dia meson Samareias kai Galilaias):** Sinaiticus reads ΔΙΑ ΜΕΣΟΝ ΣΑΜΑΡΕΙΑΣ ΚΑΙ ΓΑΛΙΛΑΙΑΣ. The geographical notice is unusual — one would expect Galilee to be mentioned before Samaria when traveling south toward Jerusalem. The phrase ΔΙΑ ΜΕΣΟΝ ("through the midst, along the border between") suggests travel along the boundary region where Samaritan and Galilean territories meet, explaining the mixed company of the ten lepers in the next verse. Luke's travel narrative (begun at 9:51) periodically reminds the reader of the Jerusalem destination (ΠΟΡΕΥΕΣΘΑΙ ΕΙΣ ΙΕΡΟΥΣΑΛΗΜ), investing every episode with the gravity of the approaching passion.

8. **17:12 — "ten leprous men" (deka leproi andres):** Sinaiticus reads ΔΕΚΑ ΛΕΠΡΟΙ ΑΝΔΡΕΣ. The number ten may be significant: ten was the minimum number for a synagogue assembly (minyan), and ten lepers constituted a community of outcasts — their disease made them a congregation of the excluded. The verb ΑΠΗΝΤΗΣΑΝ ("met him") and the notice ΟΙ ΕΣΤΗΣΑΝ ΠΟΡΡΩΘΕΝ ("who stood at a distance") reflect the Levitical requirement that lepers maintain separation from the healthy (Lev 13:45-46). Their distance is both legal and medical — they shout because they cannot approach. The disease ΛΕΠΡΑ in the ancient world encompassed various severe skin conditions, not only Hansen's disease (modern leprosy), and rendered the sufferer ritually unclean and socially dead.

9. **17:13 — "Jesus, Master" (Iēsou epistata):** Sinaiticus reads ΙΗΣΟΥ ΕΠΙΣΤΑΤΑ. The vocative ΕΠΙΣΤΑΤΑ ("Master, Commander") is used exclusively in Luke's Gospel — it denotes one who stands over others in authority. The lepers address Jesus by name and title, indicating prior knowledge of his healing ministry. Their plea ΕΛΕΗΣΟΝ ΗΜΑΣ ("have mercy on us") uses the aorist imperative of ΕΛΕΕΩ — a decisive act of mercy, not a process. The cry for ΕΛΕΟΣ ("mercy") is the fundamental prayer of the helpless: it claims no merit, offers no bargain, but throws itself entirely upon the compassion of the one addressed.

10. **17:14 — "Go, show yourselves to the priests" (poreuthentes epideixate heautous tois hiereusin):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΟΡΕΥΘΕΝΤΕΣ ΕΠΙΔΕΙΞΑΤΕ ΕΑΥΤΟΥΣ ΤΟΙΣ ΙΕΡΕΥΣΙΝ. Jesus commands what Leviticus 14:2-32 requires of the healed leper: priestly inspection and certification of cleanness. The remarkable feature is that the healing has not yet occurred — the lepers are sent to the priests while still leprous, requiring them to act in faith on a word of command alone. The phrase ΕΝ ΤΩ ΥΠΑΓΕΙΝ ΑΥΤΟΥΣ ΕΚΑΘΑΡΙΣΘΗΣΑΝ ("as they were going, they were cleansed") locates the miracle in the act of obedient departure. The aorist passive ΕΚΑΘΑΡΙΣΘΗΣΑΝ ("they were cleansed") uses the cultic vocabulary of ritual purification — their healing is simultaneously medical and ceremonial. The plural ΤΟΙΣ ΙΕΡΕΥΣΙΝ ("the priests") rather than the singular may reflect the mixed company: Jewish lepers would go to the Jerusalem priests, while the Samaritan would go to a Samaritan priest on Mount Gerizim.

11. **17:15-16 — "And he was a Samaritan" (kai autos ēn Samareitēs):** Sinaiticus reads ΚΑΙ ΑΥΤΟΣ ΗΝ ΣΑΜΑΡΕΙΤΗΣ. The delayed identification is a masterful narrative technique — Luke withholds the man's ethnicity until the climax. The sequence of the Samaritan's response is fourfold: he SAW (ΙΔΩΝ) that he was healed, he TURNED BACK (ΥΠΕΣΤΡΕΨΕΝ), he GLORIFIED God (ΔΟΞΑΖΩΝ ΤΟΝ ΘΕΟΝ) with a loud voice, and he FELL (ΕΠΕΣΕΝ ΕΠΙ ΠΡΟΣΩΠΟΝ) at Jesus's feet GIVING THANKS (ΕΥΧΑΡΙΣΤΩΝ). The verb ΕΥΧΑΡΙΣΤΕΩ ("to give thanks") — from which English "Eucharist" derives — appears here in significant conjunction with falling at Jesus's feet: thanksgiving is rendered to Jesus as to God. The notice ΚΑΙ ΑΥΤΟΣ ΗΝ ΣΑΜΑΡΕΙΤΗΣ ("and he was a Samaritan") is the devastating final clause: the religious outsider, the heretic, the one with the least theological training and the most reason to be indifferent, is the only one who returns.

12. **17:17-18 — "this foreigner" (ho allogenēs houtos):** Sinaiticus reads Ο ΑΛΛΟΓΕΝΗΣ ΟΥΤΟΣ. The word ΑΛΛΟΓΕΝΗΣ ("of another race, foreigner") occurs only here in the New Testament but was inscribed on the barrier wall of the Jerusalem temple warning Gentiles not to enter the inner courts on pain of death. Jesus applies this exclusionary term to the Samaritan with evident irony: the one whom the temple excludes is the one who renders proper worship. The three questions of Jesus (Were not the ten cleansed? Where are the nine? Were none found returning to give glory to God except this foreigner?) form an escalating indictment: the scope of the miracle (ten), the scope of the ingratitude (nine), and the scandal of the one grateful response (a foreigner).

13. **17:19 — "your faith has saved you" (hē pistis sou sesōken se):** Sinaiticus reads Η ΠΙΣΤΙΣ ΣΟΥ ΣΕΣΩΚΕΝ ΣΕ. The perfect tense ΣΕΣΩΚΕΝ ("has saved," with continuing result) uses ΣΩΖΩ, which encompasses both physical healing and spiritual salvation. All ten were ΕΚΑΘΑΡΙΣΘΗΣΑΝ ("cleansed"); only the Samaritan is told ΣΕΣΩΚΕΝ ΣΕ ("has saved you"). The distinction implies that the nine received physical healing but the Samaritan received something more — salvation in its fullest sense. The imperative ΑΝΑΣΤΑΣ ΠΟΡΕΥΟΥ ("rise and go") echoes the language used after other healings (5:23-24; 7:50; 8:48), establishing a pattern: faith, expressed in gratitude and worship, is the gateway to salvation beyond mere cure.

14. **17:20-21 — "The kingdom of God does not come with observation" (ouk erchetai hē basileia tou Theou meta paratērēseōs):** Sinaiticus reads ΟΥΚ ΕΡΧΕΤΑΙ Η ΒΑΣΙΛΕΙΑ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ ΜΕΤΑ ΠΑΡΑΤΗΡΗΣΕΩΣ. The noun ΠΑΡΑΤΗΡΗΣΙΣ ("observation, watching") occurs only here in the New Testament — from ΠΑΡΑΤΗΡΕΩ ("to watch closely, to observe scrupulously"), it denotes the kind of anxious scrutiny that watches for signs and calculates timetables. The kingdom cannot be tracked by such methods. The declaration ΕΝΤΟΣ ΥΜΩΝ ("in the midst of you" or "within you") is famously ambiguous: ΕΝΤΟΣ can mean "within" (interior, spiritual) or "among, in the midst of" (exterior, present). Given that Jesus addresses Pharisees who do not believe in him, "in the midst of you" (i.e., the kingdom is already present among you in the person and ministry of Jesus) is the more probable reading. The formal translation renders "in the midst of you" to preserve the spatial rather than psychological sense of ΕΝΤΟΣ.

15. **17:22 — "one of the days of the Son of Man" (mian tōn hēmerōn tou huiou tou anthrōpou):** Sinaiticus reads ΜΙΑΝ ΤΩΝ ΗΜΕΡΩΝ ΤΟΥ ΥΙΟΥ ΤΟΥ ΑΝΘΡΩΠΟΥ. Jesus now turns from the Pharisees (v. 20) to the disciples (v. 22), shifting from the kingdom's present reality to its future consummation. The phrase ΜΙΑΝ ΤΩΝ ΗΜΕΡΩΝ ("one of the days") is partitive — they will long for even a single day of the Son of Man's manifest presence. The verb ΕΠΙΘΥΜΗΣΕΤΕ ("you will desire, you will long for") is strong — it denotes passionate, unfulfilled yearning. ΚΑΙ ΟΥΚ ΟΨΕΣΘΕ ("and you will not see it") declares that this longing will remain unsatisfied during the interim period before the Parousia. The logion prepares the disciples for an era of absence in which they must resist false claims of the kingdom's arrival (v. 23).

16. **17:24 — "as the lightning" (hōsper hē astrapē):** Sinaiticus reads ΩΣΠΕΡ Η ΑΣΤΡΑΠΗ. The simile of lightning establishes two qualities of the Son of Man's coming: instantaneity and universality. Lightning does not progress gradually from one horizon to another — it illuminates the entire sky simultaneously. The phrase ΕΚ ΤΗΣ ΥΠΟ ΤΟΝ ΟΥΡΑΝΟΝ ΕΙΣ ΤΗΝ ΥΠ ΟΥΡΑΝΟΝ ("from one part under heaven to the other part under heaven") uses the cosmic scope of the firmament as the measure of visibility. There will be no need to search for the Son of Man; his appearing will be as unmistakable as a lightning flash that fills the entire sky. The phrase ΕΝ ΤΗ ΗΜΕΡΑ ΑΥΤΟΥ ("in his day") is present in Sinaiticus, though some manuscripts omit it.

17. **17:25 — "first he must suffer many things" (prōton dei auton polla pathein):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΡΩΤΟΝ ΔΕΙ ΑΥΤΟΝ ΠΟΛΛΑ ΠΑΘΕΙΝ. The adverb ΠΡΩΤΟΝ ("first") and the verb ΔΕΙ ("it is necessary, it must be") together assert divine necessity with temporal priority: before the glorious appearing of v. 24, suffering must come. The verb ΠΑΘΕΙΝ ("to suffer") and the phrase ΑΠΟΔΟΚΙΜΑΣΘΗΝΑΙ ΑΠΟ ΤΗΣ ΓΕΝΕΑΣ ΤΑΥΤΗΣ ("to be rejected by this generation") echo the passion predictions of 9:22 and 9:44. The insertion of this passion saying into an eschatological discourse is characteristically Lukan — the cross is the necessary precondition of the Parousia. No road to glory bypasses Golgotha.

18. **17:26-27 — "the days of Noah" (hai hēmerai Nōe):** Sinaiticus reads ΑΙ ΗΜΕΡΑΙ ΝΩΕ. The comparison with Noah's generation establishes the pattern: ordinary human activities — ΗΣΘΙΟΝ ("they were eating"), ΕΠΙΝΟΝ ("they were drinking"), ΕΓΑΜΟΥΝ ("they were marrying"), ΕΓΑΜΙΖΟΝΤΟ ("they were being given in marriage") — continued without interruption until sudden catastrophe. The imperfect tenses convey habitual, ongoing activity: life proceeding in its customary rhythms. The verb ΕΙΣΗΛΘΕΝ ("entered") marks the pivot — ΑΧΡΙ ΗΣ ΗΜΕΡΑΣ ("until which day") identifies a single, decisive moment when judgment fell. ΗΛΘΕΝ Ο ΚΑΤΑΚΛΥΣΜΟΣ ΚΑΙ ΑΠΩΛΕΣΕΝ ΠΑΝΤΑΣ ("the flood came and destroyed them all") — the ΚΑΤΑΚΛΥΣΜΟΣ ("cataclysm, deluge") arrives without graduated warning. The point is not the sinfulness of eating and drinking but the oblivious normalcy of life that takes no account of coming judgment.

19. **17:28-29 — "the days of Lot" (hai hēmerai Lōt):** Sinaiticus reads ΑΙ ΗΜΕΡΑΙ ΛΩΤ. The Lot parallel expands the catalog of ordinary activities: ΗΓΟΡΑΖΟΝ ("they were buying"), ΕΠΩΛΟΥΝ ("they were selling"), ΕΦΥΤΕΥΟΝ ("they were planting"), ΩΚΟΔΟΜΟΥΝ ("they were building") — commerce and agriculture, the foundations of civilized life. The verb ΕΒΡΕΞΕΝ ("it rained") takes ΠΥΡ ΚΑΙ ΘΕΙΟΝ ("fire and brimstone") as its object — an inversion of nature where the sky rains destruction instead of water. ΑΠ ΟΥΡΑΝΟΥ ("from heaven") identifies the source as divine judgment. ΚΑΙ ΑΠΩΛΕΣΕΝ ΠΑΝΤΑΣ ("and destroyed them all") repeats the totality of the Noah judgment — ΠΑΝΤΑΣ ("all") admits no exceptions among those who remained.

20. **17:31 — "let him not come down to take them up" (mē katabatō arai auta):** Sinaiticus reads ΜΗ ΚΑΤΑΒΑΤΩ ΑΡΑΙ ΑΥΤΑ. The housetop (ΔΩΜΑ) was the flat roof of a Palestinian house, accessible by an external staircase. The warning not to descend to retrieve possessions (ΤΑ ΣΚΕΥΗ, "goods, vessels") from inside the house envisions a moment of such urgency that even the time required to enter one's own home is too much. Similarly, the man in the field (ΕΝ ΑΓΡΩ) must not turn back (ΜΗ ΕΠΙΣΤΡΕΨΑΤΩ ΕΙΣ ΤΑ ΟΠΙΣΩ, "let him not turn to the things behind"). The language echoes the flight from Sodom (Gen 19:17: "Do not look behind you").

21. **17:32 — "Remember the wife of Lot" (mnēmoneuete tēs gynaikos Lōt):** Sinaiticus reads ΜΝΗΜΟΝΕΥΕΤΕ ΤΗΣ ΓΥΝΑΙΚΟΣ ΛΩΤ. This is the shortest verse in the eschatological discourse — three words in Greek. The imperative ΜΝΗΜΟΝΕΥΕΤΕ ("remember") demands active recall of Genesis 19:26: Lot's wife looked back and became a pillar of salt. She is the embodiment of verse 31 — the one who turned to the things behind. The brevity of the command is itself instructive: no elaboration is needed; the story is universally known and its lesson self-evident. Attachment to the old life in the moment of deliverance is fatal.

22. **17:33 — "whoever shall lose it shall preserve it alive" (hos d' an apolesē zōogonēsei autēn):** Sinaiticus reads ΟΣ Δ ΑΝ ΑΠΟΛΕΣΗ ΖΩΟΓΟΝΗΣΕΙ ΑΥΤΗΝ. The verb ΖΩΟΓΟΝΕΩ ("to preserve alive, to give life to") is rare and vivid — from ΖΩΗ ("life") + ΓΕΝΝΑΩ/ΓΙΝΟΜΑΙ ("to beget, to come into being"), it literally means "to bring to life." It occurs in the LXX for the preservation of living things (e.g., Exod 1:17-18, the midwives who preserved the Hebrew children alive). The paradox is absolute: ΖΗΤΗΣΑΙ ΤΗΝ ΨΥΧΗΝ ΑΥΤΟΥ ΠΕΡΙΠΟΙΗΣΑΣΘΑΙ ("to seek to preserve his life") results in ΑΠΟΛΕΣΕΙ ("he shall lose it"), while ΑΠΟΛΕΣΗ ("shall lose it") results in ΖΩΟΓΟΝΗΣΕΙ ("shall preserve it alive"). The chiastic structure (seek-preserve/lose versus lose/preserve-alive) makes the reversal inescapable.

23. **17:34-35 — "two upon one bed ... two women grinding" (dyo epi klinēs mias ... dyo alēthousai):** Sinaiticus reads ΔΥΟ ΕΠΙ ΚΛΙΝΗΣ ΜΙΑΣ and ΔΥΟ ΑΛΗΘΟΥΣΑΙ ΕΠΙ ΤΟ ΑΥΤΟ. The scene shifts from cosmic panorama to intimate domestic settings. ΤΑΥΤΗ ΤΗ ΝΥΚΤΙ ("in that night") establishes the temporal setting. Two on one bed (ΚΛΙΝΗ) — likely household members or servants sharing a sleeping pallet — are separated by judgment: ΠΑΡΑΛΗΜΦΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ ("shall be taken") and ΑΦΕΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ ("shall be left"). The passive verbs suggest divine action. In verse 35, two women grinding together (ΑΛΗΘΟΥΣΑΙ ΕΠΙ ΤΟ ΑΥΤΟ) at a hand-mill represents the most mundane of daily labors. The same division occurs. The point is the absolute discrimination of judgment: proximity of life circumstance does not guarantee identity of destiny.

24. **17:36 — Verse absent from Sinaiticus:** Codex Sinaiticus does not contain verse 36 ("Two shall be in the field; the one shall be taken, and the other left"), which appears in later manuscripts as a harmonization with Matthew 24:40. The formal translation follows the Sinaiticus text and omits this verse, noting its absence. This omission is shared by Vaticanus (B) and other early witnesses, confirming its secondary character.

25. **17:37 — "Where the body is, there also will the eagles be gathered together" (hopou to sōma, ekei kai hoi aetoi episynachthēsontai):** Sinaiticus reads ΟΠΟΥ ΤΟ ΣΩΜΑ ΕΚΕΙ ΚΑΙ ΟΙ ΑΕΤΟΙ ΕΠΙΣΥΝΑΧΘΗΣΟΝΤΑΙ. The disciples' question ΠΟΥΚΥΡΙΕ ("Where, Lord?") asks for the location of these events. Jesus responds with a proverb rather than a geographic answer. The word ΣΩΜΑ ("body") rather than ΠΤΩΜΑ ("corpse") is the Sinaiticus reading, though the meaning is the same — a dead body. ΑΕΤΟΙ may denote eagles or vultures (both are covered by the Greek term); contextually, vultures circling carrion is the more fitting image. The proverb's meaning is: just as vultures inevitably find the carcass, so the judgment of the Son of Man will inevitably find its object. No coordinates are given because none are needed — the judgment will be self-locating, as unmistakable and unerring as the vulture's descent upon the dead.

### Translation Philosophy

1. **Formal equivalence across three discourse units:** Luke 17 comprises three distinct teaching blocks — instructions on community life (vv. 1-10), the healing of the ten lepers (vv. 11-19), and eschatological discourse (vv. 20-37) — each addressed to different audiences (disciples, lepers, Pharisees, then disciples again). The formal translation preserves the transitional markers (ΚΑΙ ΕΙΠΕΝ, "and he said"; ΚΑΙ ΕΓΕΝΕΤΟ, "and it came to pass"; ΕΠΕΡΩΤΗΘΕΙΣ ΔΕ, "and having been asked") that signal these shifts, maintaining the episodic structure without smoothing the seams.

2. **Preserving the Sinaiticus text at verse 36:** The most significant textual decision in this chapter is the omission of verse 36, which Codex Sinaiticus does not contain. Rather than inserting the verse from later manuscripts with brackets, the formal translation follows the principle of translating the Sinaiticus text as transmitted, with a notation at the appropriate location. This creates the verse numbering 34, 35, 37 — a visible reminder that all verse numbering is a medieval convenience imposed upon the manuscript tradition.

3. **The Samaritan's thanksgiving and Christological worship:** The formal translation preserves the precise vocabulary of worship in the leper narrative: ΔΟΞΑΖΩΝ ΤΟΝ ΘΕΟΝ ("glorifying God") paired with ΕΠΕΣΕΝ ΕΠΙ ΠΡΟΣΩΠΟΝ ΠΑΡΑ ΤΟΥΣ ΠΟΔΑΣ ΑΥΤΟΥ ΕΥΧΑΡΙΣΤΩΝ ΑΥΤΩ ("fell upon his face at his feet, giving thanks to him"). The shift from glorifying God to thanking Jesus at Jesus's feet raises a Christological question that Luke leaves for the reader to resolve: is the thanksgiving directed to Jesus as to God, or is Jesus the mediator of divine glory? The formal translation preserves the ambiguity by translating the pronouns precisely as they stand.

4. **Eschatological urgency and the imperfect tenses:** The formal translation retains the imperfect tenses in the Noah and Lot comparisons ("they were eating, they were drinking, they were marrying") rather than simplifying to simple past. The imperfect conveys the habitual, ongoing character of these activities — life in its endless routine, unaware of the approaching catastrophe. This grammatical precision is essential to the theological point: judgment interrupts normalcy, not depravity.

5. **Continuity with Luke 16:** The chapter transition from 16:31 to 17:1 is seamless in Sinaiticus — there is no chapter division in the manuscript. The teaching on stumbling-blocks (17:1-2) follows immediately upon the parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus, and the theme of responsibility toward "little ones" (17:2) connects to the theme of responsibility toward the poor (16:19-31). The formal translation preserves the connective ΚΑΙ ("and") that binds 17:1 to the preceding discourse, resisting the chapter division's implication of a fresh start.
